planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall, root stem cuttings, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; shear catmint back by one-third to one-half immediately after the first main flush of flowers fades to promote a second flush of bloom in late summer — this deadheading and shearing is the primary cultural key to prolonging the blooming season.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; dwarf catmint is a low-growing Caucasian perennial that forms billowing, soft mounds of small, gray-green, aromatic, toothed, hairy leaves and produces dense, continuous spikes of small, tubular, lavender-blue to violet flowers in late spring and early summer, creating one of the most attractive and bee-friendly edging plants available for the perennial border. The soft, hazy, silver-gray foliage and lavender-blue flower combination creates a classic cottage garden look that is attractive even out of flower. Bumblebees and honeybees work catmint flowers with unusual intensity. If sheared back by half after the first main flush fades in early summer, it produces a second flush of bloom in August–September. Drought-tolerant and long-lived. The named cultivar 'Walker's Low' (despite its misleading name, reaches 18–24 inches) is the most widely planted selection for its extra-vigorous, large-flowered display. Distinguished from the larger Nepeta x faassenii by being the true species and setting viable seed.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring; perennial honesty blooms most freely in the dappled light of open deciduous woodland where it receives good spring light before the canopy leafs out, then is protected from summer desiccation by the shade.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; perennial honesty is a European shade woodland perennial — the long-lived, truly perennial species of the honesty family — that differs from the common biennial honesty (Lunaria annua) in being a genuine perennial that returns for many years without replanting, in producing intensely sweet, lilac-scented flowers (rather than the unscented flowers of the biennial), and in having oval rather than round seed pods. In late spring the plant produces tall stems bearing dense clusters of small, four-petaled, lilac-purple to white, powerfully honey-and-violet-fragrant flowers above large, heart-shaped, toothed leaves. After flowering, the flattened, elliptical, papery, silvery seed pods develop and dry to a translucent, pearly-white, decorative finish that is highly ornamental for dried arrangements. It grows naturally in the dappled light of deciduous woodland edges and is genuinely shade-tolerant — one of few flowering perennials that produces good flower and seed pod displays in partial to full shade. Spreads gradually by self-seeding.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring or fall; Siberian bergenia is one of the most cold-tolerant evergreen shade groundcover plants available and is fully hardy to zone 2 (-50°F) — invaluable for cold-climate shade gardens where most broadleaf evergreens are not reliably hardy.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in moist, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Siberian bergenia is an Asian shade evergreen perennial renowned primarily for its exceptional cold-hardiness (to zone 2, or approximately -50°F) and its ability to maintain its large, rounded, leathery, glossy, dark-green leaves through the harshest winters with minimal damage — the massive, cabbage-like, evergreen leaves provide bold textural interest throughout the year, and the foliage develops rich bronze, red, and burgundy tones in cold weather, particularly when planted in a position receiving some winter sun. In late winter and early spring (February–April depending on zone), bright-pink to magenta, bell-shaped flowers on thick, reddish stalks emerge directly from the crown, often appearing before the snow has fully melted. Siberian bergenia is one of the two or three most cold-tolerant evergreen shade groundcovers available for temperate gardens and is an essential plant for shaded positions in zone 2–4 gardens where most competing shade groundcovers are not reliably hardy. Spreads steadily by thick, creeping rhizomes to form dense weed-excluding colonies.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring or fall; purple bergenia's distinctive red-purple winter coloration is most vivid when the plant receives some cold and some winter sunlight — deep full shade and consistently mild winters suppress the purple coloration.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in moist, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; purple bergenia is a Himalayan shade evergreen perennial distinguished from other bergenia species by its exceptional and consistent winter foliage coloring — from autumn through winter, the large, rounded, leathery, shiny leaves turn a deep, vivid, wine-red to purple-red that intensifies with cold weather and sunlight exposure, creating one of the finest winter foliage color effects available from any shade-tolerant evergreen groundcover plant. In spring the foliage gradually returns to dark green as temperatures warm. The flowers, produced in March–April on upright, red-stalked stems, are deep rosy-pink to magenta and particularly vivid against the purplish-red foliage of late winter. Purple bergenia is one of the primary species in the background of many Bergenia hybrid cultivars bred specifically for enhanced winter foliage coloring, including 'Bressingham Ruby' and 'Sunningdale'. It is among the most ornamental species of the genus for four-season interest, combining spring flowers, summer foliage groundcover, and rich winter purple coloring into a single, low-maintenance, long-lived evergreen.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; tree spinach self-seeds prolifically and, once introduced to a garden, typically maintains itself as a self-renewing annual — thin seedlings to the desired spacing and harvest thinnings as baby greens.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; tree spinach (magenta spreen, giant goosefoot) is a Himalayan annual of the goosefoot family grown as an ornamental and edible plant — it grows remarkably tall (4–10 feet) for an annual leafy vegetable, producing large, deep-green, arrowhead-shaped leaves that are eaten raw or cooked in exactly the same way as spinach or lamb's quarters, with a mild, pleasant, slightly mineral flavor. The ornamental distinction of the species is the vivid, magenta-pink meal (a waxy powder similar to that on lamb's quarters) that coats the youngest emerging leaves, shoot tips, and stems, creating a strikingly colorful display at the growing tips that fades on older leaves to plain green. The plant self-seeds freely, ensuring persistent garden colonies once established. It grows rapidly, is tolerant of partial shade, is highly nutritious (rich in protein, calcium, and iron, like other Chenopodium species), and provides both an ornamental feature and a productive edible leafy green from a single plant. An underused dual-purpose annual for the edible landscape.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
